front: What is the formal definition of a finite set?
back: A set A is finite if there exists a bijection (one-to-one and onto function) from A to the set {1, 2, ..., n} for some natural number n. The number n is called the cardinality of A.

front: What is the defining property of an infinite set?
back: A set is infinite if it is not finite. Equivalently, a set A is infinite if there exists a proper subset B ⊂ A (B is contained in A but not equal to A) such that a bijection exists between A and B.

front: What key property distinguishes an infinite set in a metric space regarding limit points?
back: In a metric space, a finite set has no limit points. Conversely, if a set has a limit point, it must be infinite. However, an infinite set does not necessarily have to have a limit point (e.g., the set of integers in ℝ with the standard metric).

A set A is Dedekind-infinite if it can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with a proper subset of itself. This definition is equivalent to the standard definition of infinite if the Axiom of Choice is assumed.

front: Why is the concept of finite and infinite sets fundamental in metric spaces?
back: Properties like compactness, which is central in metric space theory, are defined using finite subcovers. A set is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Understanding finiteness is therefore crucial for analyzing convergence, continuity, and boundedness.
